title: 15_fp16_underflow.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Make the Half-Precision Fine-Tune Learn Again

The trust-and-safety team fine-tunes a 4-layer transformer encoder into a toxicity classifier every week. To fit batch 256 on the shared 16 GB GPU and pick up the tensor-core speedup, the run was converted to half precision — weights, inputs, activations, everything fp16. Memory halved and throughput nearly doubled, exactly as advertised.

But the training gate is now red. After 300 steps the loss has crawled from 0.6929 to 0.6924 — still a coin flip — while the fp32 baseline reaches 0.29 on the identical schedule, data, and learning rate. A probe added during triage reports that 58 of the model's 70 parameter tensors end training with gradients that are *exactly* zero, and the deeper the layer, the deader it is. The forward pass is healthy and the labels check out; the training signal is being destroyed somewhere between the loss and the weights. Find out where the numbers go to die.
initialCode:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from moderation.data import get_train_batches

torch.manual_seed(0)
model = torch.load("toxicity_encoder_init.pt", weights_only=False)
model = model.cuda().half()
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=2e-4)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

losses = []
for x, y in get_train_batches(steps=300):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(x.cuda().half())
    loss = loss_fn(logits, y.cuda())
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    losses.append(loss.item())

grad_norms = [p.grad.float().norm().item() for p in model.parameters()]
zero = sum(g == 0.0 for g in grad_norms)
print(f"first-layer grad norm: {grad_norms[0]:.2e}, head grad norm: {grad_norms[-1]:.2e}")
print(f"all-zero grad tensors: {zero}/{len(grad_norms)}")
print(f"loss: step 1 = {losses[0]:.4f}, step 300 = {losses[-1]:.4f}")
assert losses[-1] < losses[0] - 0.10, f"no learning progress: {losses[0]:.4f} -> {losses[-1]:.4f} (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)"
traceback:
first-layer grad norm: 0.00e+00, head grad norm: 2.44e-04
all-zero grad tensors: 58/70
loss: step 1 = 0.6929, step 300 = 0.6924
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 25
     23 print(f"all-zero grad tensors: {zero}/{len(grad_norms)}")
     24 print(f"loss: step 1 = {losses[0]:.4f}, step 300 = {losses[-1]:.4f}")
---> 25 assert losses[-1] < losses[0] - 0.10, f"no learning progress: {losses[0]:.4f} -> {losses[-1]:.4f} (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)"

AssertionError: no learning progress: 0.6929 -> 0.6924 (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)
correctOutput:
first-layer grad norm: 1.83e-06, head grad norm: 2.59e-04
all-zero grad tensors: 0/70
loss: step 1 = 0.6931, step 300 = 0.2876

options.label: Compute the loss in float32 by casting the logits before CrossEntropyLoss
options.patchCode:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from moderation.data import get_train_batches

torch.manual_seed(0)
model = torch.load("toxicity_encoder_init.pt", weights_only=False)
model = model.cuda().half()
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=2e-4)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

losses = []
for x, y in get_train_batches(steps=300):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(x.cuda().half())
    loss = loss_fn(logits.float(), y.cuda())
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    losses.append(loss.item())

grad_norms = [p.grad.float().norm().item() for p in model.parameters()]
zero = sum(g == 0.0 for g in grad_norms)
print(f"first-layer grad norm: {grad_norms[0]:.2e}, head grad norm: {grad_norms[-1]:.2e}")
print(f"all-zero grad tensors: {zero}/{len(grad_norms)}")
print(f"loss: step 1 = {losses[0]:.4f}, step 300 = {losses[-1]:.4f}")
assert losses[-1] < losses[0] - 0.10, f"no learning progress: {losses[0]:.4f} -> {losses[-1]:.4f} (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)"
options.resultLog:
first-layer grad norm: 0.00e+00, head grad norm: 2.61e-04
all-zero grad tensors: 58/70
loss: step 1 = 0.6931, step 300 = 0.6927
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 25
     23 print(f"all-zero grad tensors: {zero}/{len(grad_norms)}")
     24 print(f"loss: step 1 = {losses[0]:.4f}, step 300 = {losses[-1]:.4f}")
---> 25 assert losses[-1] < losses[0] - 0.10, f"no learning progress: {losses[0]:.4f} -> {losses[-1]:.4f} (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)"

AssertionError: no learning progress: 0.6931 -> 0.6927 (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)
options.rationale: The cast happens at the wrong end of the graph. The loss (~0.69) was never the number in danger — it is the chain-rule products *behind* it that shrink below fp16's ~6e-8 floor, and those are still computed in fp16 because every weight and activation still is (the `.half()` on line 7 of this patch is untouched). The fp32 loss hands a healthy gradient to the last layer, which then disintegrates on its way down exactly as before: 58 of 70 tensors still finish at exactly zero and the loss still flatlines. Computing the loss in fp32 is good hygiene *inside* a real mixed-precision setup, but on its own it scales nothing and rescues nothing.

options.label: Multiply the loss by a fixed 1024 before backward to lift the small gradients
options.patchCode:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from moderation.data import get_train_batches

torch.manual_seed(0)
model = torch.load("toxicity_encoder_init.pt", weights_only=False)
model = model.cuda().half()
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=2e-4)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

losses = []
for x, y in get_train_batches(steps=300):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(x.cuda().half())
    loss = loss_fn(logits, y.cuda())
    (loss * 1024).backward()
    optimizer.step()
    losses.append(loss.item())

grad_norms = [p.grad.float().norm().item() for p in model.parameters()]
zero = sum(g == 0.0 for g in grad_norms)
print(f"first-layer grad norm: {grad_norms[0]:.2e}, head grad norm: {grad_norms[-1]:.2e}")
print(f"all-zero grad tensors: {zero}/{len(grad_norms)}")
print(f"loss: step 1 = {losses[0]:.4f}, step 300 = {losses[-1]:.4f}")
assert losses[-1] < losses[0] - 0.10, f"no learning progress: {losses[0]:.4f} -> {losses[-1]:.4f} (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)"
options.resultLog:
first-layer grad norm: nan, head grad norm: nan
all-zero grad tensors: 0/70
loss: step 1 = 0.6929, step 300 = nan
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 25
     23 print(f"all-zero grad tensors: {zero}/{len(grad_norms)}")
     24 print(f"loss: step 1 = {losses[0]:.4f}, step 300 = {losses[-1]:.4f}")
---> 25 assert losses[-1] < losses[0] - 0.10, f"no learning progress: {losses[0]:.4f} -> {losses[-1]:.4f} (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)"

AssertionError: no learning progress: 0.6929 -> nan (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)
options.rationale: Scaling the loss is genuinely half of the right idea — by linearity of differentiation, ×1024 lifts every gradient, and the 1e-8 stragglers clear fp16's floor. But a real scaler divides the gradients back down before the optimizer touches them; this patch steps on gradients that are still 1024× too big, which is a silent 1024× learning rate. The fine-tune detonates within a few dozen steps: weights blow up, activations sail past fp16's 65,504 ceiling into inf, the loss goes NaN — and unlike GradScaler there is no inf-check to skip the poisoned steps, so the corruption is written straight into the weights. Right mechanism, missing the unscale, the safety valve, and the fp32 master weights underneath.

options.label: Wrap the forward pass in torch.amp.autocast and drive backward/step through a GradScaler
options.patchCode:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from moderation.data import get_train_batches

torch.manual_seed(0)
model = torch.load("toxicity_encoder_init.pt", weights_only=False)
model = model.cuda()
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=2e-4)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
scaler = torch.amp.GradScaler("cuda")

losses = []
for x, y in get_train_batches(steps=300):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    with torch.amp.autocast("cuda"):
        logits = model(x.cuda())
        loss = loss_fn(logits, y.cuda())
    scaler.scale(loss).backward()
    scaler.step(optimizer)
    scaler.update()
    losses.append(loss.item())

grad_norms = [p.grad.float().norm().item() for p in model.parameters()]
zero = sum(g == 0.0 for g in grad_norms)
print(f"first-layer grad norm: {grad_norms[0]:.2e}, head grad norm: {grad_norms[-1]:.2e}")
print(f"all-zero grad tensors: {zero}/{len(grad_norms)}")
print(f"loss: step 1 = {losses[0]:.4f}, step 300 = {losses[-1]:.4f}")
assert losses[-1] < losses[0] - 0.10, f"no learning progress: {losses[0]:.4f} -> {losses[-1]:.4f} (fp32 baseline reaches 0.29)"
options.resultLog:
first-layer grad norm: 1.83e-06, head grad norm: 2.59e-04
all-zero grad tensors: 0/70
loss: step 1 = 0.6931, step 300 = 0.2876
options.rationale: `autocast` assigns precision per operation — matmuls run in fp16 for the tensor cores; softmax, norms, and the loss stay in fp32 — while the parameters themselves remain fp32 master weights. `GradScaler` closes the range gap: `scale(loss).backward()` multiplies every gradient by a dynamic factor (~2^16) so nothing flushes to zero in the fp16 backward, `step()` unscales in fp32 and skips any step containing inf/nan, and `update()` retunes the factor. Gradients now reach all 70 tensors, and updates land on fp32 weights where a 2e-8 increment actually accumulates: loss falls 0.6931 → 0.2876, matching the fp32 baseline while activations — the bulk of the memory bill — stay fp16.

hints: Rank everything in one training step by magnitude: activations and loss around 1e0, weights around 1e-2, gradients from 1e-4 at the head down to 1e-8 in early layers, and weight updates (lr × grad) smaller still. fp16 holds normal numbers down to ~6.1e-5, stretches subnormals to ~6e-8, and flushes everything below that to exact zero. Which numbers in this loop live below the floor of the format they are stored in?

Lines 7–16: line 7 casts every weight to fp16 and line 14 feeds the model fp16 inputs, so the backward on line 16 computes the entire chain rule in fp16 — early-layer gradients around 1e-8 flush to zero before the optimizer ever sees them. The survivors don't help either: the step adds a ~2e-8 update onto fp16 weights whose representable spacing near 0.01 is ~8e-6, so the addition rounds straight back to the old weight. Nothing lifts the small values out of the underflow zone before backward, and no fp32 copy of the weights exists to absorb tiny updates after it.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
fp16 spends 5 bits on the exponent and 10 on the mantissa: the smallest normal number is ~6.1e-5, subnormals limp on to ~6e-8, and everything below that flushes to exact zero. Casting the model and its inputs to fp16 (lines 7 and 14) forces the backward pass to compute every chain-rule product in fp16 as well. Gradients are the smallest numbers in the loop, and they shrink as they propagate backwards — by the early layers of the encoder they sit around 1e-7 to 1e-8, below the flush-to-zero floor, which is why 58 of 70 tensors finish with gradients that are exactly 0.0 and why the deepest layers die first. The classifier head's gradients (~1e-4) survive, but a second mechanism kills them at the update: `optimizer.step()` computes `w - lr*g` ≈ 0.01 − 2e-8 in fp16, where the representable spacing around 0.01 is ~8e-6, so the subtraction rounds back to the original weight. No gradient below, no weight movement above: the loss pins at 0.69 and the progress assert fires.

### Why the fix is correct
`torch.amp.autocast("cuda")` assigns precision per operation — matmuls run in fp16 for tensor-core speed while reductions, softmax, and the loss stay in fp32 — and, crucially, the parameters remain fp32 master weights. `GradScaler` then manages the dynamic range: `scaler.scale(loss).backward()` multiplies the loss (and therefore every gradient, by linearity of differentiation) by a large dynamic factor, lifting 1e-8 gradients into fp16's representable zone; `scaler.step(optimizer)` divides them back down *in fp32* before the update and skips the step entirely if any inf/nan slipped in; `scaler.update()` adapts the factor over time. Updates now land on fp32 weights, where a 2e-8 increment accumulates instead of vanishing — all 70 tensors receive gradient and the loss falls to 0.2876, matching the fp32 baseline at roughly half the activation memory.
recruiterReview: Exactly right, and you resisted both traps. Casting the loss to fp32 was the tempting one — but the loss was never the underflowing number; the chain-rule products deep in the backward were, and they were doomed the moment the weights went fp16. The principle you applied is the one I hire for: mixed precision is per-op precision assignment plus dynamic-range management, not a blanket cast — fp16 where it is fast, fp32 master weights where increments must accumulate, and a scaler to ferry gradients across the representable gap. The manual ×1024 option failing with NaN is worth filing away too: scaling without unscaling before step() is a silent 1024× learning rate. Forward-looking: on Ampere-or-newer hardware try bf16 autocast — fp32's exponent range means no scaler at all — and wire the zero-grad-tensor probe into CI so the next precision regression fails loudly at step 1 instead of after a wasted training run. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Mixed Precision Is a Contract, Not a Cast

Half precision looks like free performance: tensor cores chew through fp16 matrix multiplies several times faster than fp32, and every tensor costs half the memory. The catch is that fp16 is not just a smaller tensor — it is a smaller *number format*, and training (unlike inference) routinely produces values that fall out of it. Mixed precision training is the discipline of deciding, value by value, which format each number lives in. A blanket `.half()` skips that decision, and the resulting failure is the worst kind: no exception, no warning, just a model that silently stops learning.

### The anatomy of fp16
An fp16 number has 1 sign bit, 5 exponent bits, and 10 mantissa bits. That buys a maximum of 65,504 (above which values become `inf`), normal numbers down to ~6.1e-5, and subnormals down to ~6e-8 — below that, exact zero. So there are two cliff edges: an *overflow ceiling* at the top (softmax logits and attention scores can hit it) and an *underflow floor* at the bottom. The mantissa adds a third, quieter constraint: with 10 bits, computing `a + b` does nothing unless `b/a` is bigger than roughly 2⁻¹¹. Each of these three limits eats a different part of the training loop.

### Where training's smallest numbers live
Rank one training step's tensors by magnitude: activations and loss sit around 1e0, weights around 1e-2, gradients from ~1e-3 at the head down to 1e-7 or 1e-8 in early layers — the chain rule multiplies many small Jacobians as it walks backwards — and weight updates (learning rate × gradient) are smaller still. In fp32 all of that is comfortably representable. In fp16, the bottom of the gradient distribution falls through the 6e-8 floor and flushes to zero, layer by layer from the input side up: the network goes dead from the bottom. And the gradients that survive die at the update instead: `w - lr*g` with `lr*g / w` below ~2⁻¹¹ rounds straight back to `w`. A model can have nonzero gradients and still not move.

### The three jobs of real mixed precision
Working AMP setups — `torch.amp` in PyTorch, equivalents in JAX and TensorFlow — do three distinct jobs, and all three matter:

1. **Per-op precision (autocast)**: matmuls and convolutions run in fp16 where tensor cores pay off; range-sensitive ops — softmax, norms, reductions, the loss — run in fp32. You write one `with` block; the framework owns the op-by-op policy.
2. **Loss scaling (GradScaler)**: multiply the loss by a large, dynamically tuned factor (starting around 2¹⁶) so every gradient — scaled by the same factor, since differentiation is linear — clears the underflow floor; then *unscale in fp32 before the optimizer step*, and skip any step whose gradients overflowed to inf/nan, shrinking the factor for next time. Scaling without the unscale half is just a multiplied learning rate; scaling without the skip logic writes one overflow straight into the weights.
3. **fp32 master weights**: parameters and optimizer state stay in fp32 so that microscopic updates accumulate across steps. Low-precision copies are made per forward pass — they are never the source of truth.

### bf16 changes the trade, not the contract
bfloat16 keeps fp32's 8 exponent bits, so gradients essentially never underflow and no scaler is needed — which is why it is the default on Ampere-and-newer GPUs and on TPUs. It pays with a 7-bit mantissa: a blanket-cast bf16 model swallows weight updates even more aggressively than fp16. The master-weight rule survives every format change: compute may be low precision, accumulation may not.

### Common variations of this bug
- A blanket `model.half()` borrowed from inference code and reused for training — the original sin behind this challenge.
- Manual static loss scaling that multiplies the loss but never unscales the gradients before `step()` — a silent N× learning rate that usually diverges to NaN.
- Gradient clipping applied to still-scaled gradients: the threshold is meaningless unless `scaler.unscale_(optimizer)` runs first.
- A blanket bf16 cast: gradients flow, but updates vanish into the 7-bit mantissa without fp32 master weights, so the loss curve goes flat at a higher floor.
- Softmax, layer norm, or the loss computed in fp16 inside a custom kernel or hand-rolled cast region — overflow to inf and NaN losses instead of underflow.
- Optimizer state stored in fp16 — Adam's second moment is roughly gradient², which underflows at twice the exponent rate of the gradients themselves.
- Forgetting `scaler.update()`, so the scale never recovers after an overflow and every subsequent step is silently skipped.
